pattern: Only a maximal run of consecutive bar values can ever be removed together without leaving an unremoved bar splitting the gap, so the biggest achievable square side is the shorter of the two longest consecutive runs (plus one) among the removable horizontal and vertical bars.
hints: Removing a bar only helps if the bars around it are also removed -- a lone removed bar surrounded by un-removed ones just gives back a single unit cell, not a bigger hole.

Sort each list of removable bar positions and find the longest run of consecutive integers (values that go up by exactly 1 each step) -- a run of length L, once fully removed, opens a gap of L+1 unit-cells in that direction.
The hole can only be as square as the smaller of the two directions allows, so the answer is the minimum of the two best achievable gaps, squared.

edge_inputs.description: a single removable bar in each direction
removable bars are not consecutive, so no run longer than one exists
one direction has a long consecutive run, the other doesn't -- the shorter direction caps the square
all removable bars in both directions form one long run each
only one removable bar total in a larger grid

stress.description: random removable bar positions with several consecutive clusters
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(5, 1000)
    m = rng.randint(5, 1000)
    hcount = rng.randint(1, min(100, n))
    vcount = rng.randint(1, min(100, m))
    hBars = sorted(rng.sample(range(2, n + 2), hcount))
    vBars = sorted(rng.sample(range(2, m + 2), vcount))
    return [n, m, hBars, vBars]

complexity.time: O(hBars.length log hBars.length + vBars.length log vBars.length)
